perturbation.original_text: 5. In the event that the Recipient shall breach this Agreement, or in the event that a breach appears to be imminent, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to all legal and equitable remedies afforded it by law, and in addition may recover all reaso nable costs and attorneys' fees incurred in seeking such remedies.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. In the event that the Recipient shall breach this Agreement, or in the event that a breach appears to be imminent, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to all legal and equitable remedies afforded it by law, however the recovery of attorneys' fees will be capped at $500, regardless of actual costs.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting attorneys' fees to $500 may contradict Colorado law if the actual reasonable attorneys' fees significantly exceed this amount. Some statutes or case law may allow for the full recovery of reasonable attorneys' fees.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado Revised Statutes § 13-17-102. Attorneys fees – when prohibited.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification introduces a cap on attorneys' fees, which could contradict Colorado law. While Colorado law doesn't explicitly guarantee unlimited attorney's fees in all contract disputes, capping it at $500 regardless of actual costs may be deemed unreasonable or unenforceable, especially if the actual fees are significantly higher and deemed 'reasonable' by the court. CRS 13-17-102 addresses attorney fee awards, implying a standard of reasonableness that this fixed cap could violate.

perturbation.justification: The changed text caps attorneys' fees at $500, regardless of actual costs. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this may contradict Colorado law, particularly CRS 13-17-102, if the actual reasonable attorneys' fees are significantly higher and deemed 'reasonable' by the court. While the scraped snippets do not directly address attorney fee caps, the explanations indicate a potential conflict with the standard of reasonableness implied in Colorado law regarding attorney fee awards. This creates a contradiction because the contract's fixed cap could prevent the Disclosing Party from recovering the full amount of reasonable fees, potentially undermining their legal remedies.

perturbation.original_text: If the Confidential Information is sought by any third party, including by way of subpoena or other court process, the Recipient shall inform the Disclosing Party of the request in suffici ent time to permit the Disclosing Party to object to and, if necessary, seek court intervention to prevent the disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Confidential Information is sought by any third party, including by way of subpoena or other court process, the Recipient shall inform the Disclosing Party of the request within 24 hours. After that, the recipient can decide what to do.
perturbation.explanation: Providing only 24 hours' notice may not be 'sufficient time' under Colorado law to allow the Disclosing Party to adequately object to a subpoena or seek court intervention. Colorado Rules of Civil Procedure would dictate what is sufficient and reasonable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45
perturbation.law_explanation: This change imposes a 24-hour notification deadline, which might be insufficient for the Disclosing Party to respond to a subpoena effectively. Rule 45 of the Colorado Rules of Civil Procedure governs subpoenas and requires a 'reasonable time' for compliance and objection, depending on the circumstances. A blanket 24-hour rule may not meet this standard, particularly if complex legal issues are involved.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the original requirement of 'sufficient time' with a fixed 24-hour deadline for informing the Disclosing Party about a third-party request for confidential information. The Law Explanation states that Colorado Rules of Civil Procedure require a 'reasonable time' for compliance and objection to subpoenas, and a 24-hour rule may not meet this standard. The Perturbation Explanation also supports this by stating that 24 hours may not be 'sufficient time' under Colorado law. The original text allowed for an amount of time that ensured the Disclosing Party could object to and seek court intervention to prevent the disclosure. By changing this to a fixed 24 hour period, the changed text potentially violates Colorado law by not allowing 'reasonable time' which makes the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 6. The validity, construction and enforceability of this Agreement shall be governed in all respects by the law of the state of Colorado .
perturbation.changed_text: 6. The validity, construction and enforceability of this Agreement shall be governed in all respects by the law of the state of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware when the agreement is clearly connected to an event in Colorado (as stated in the opening paragraph) may be challenged in court if Colorado law has a more significant relationship to the agreement and the parties. Colorado choice-of-law rules may prevent the enforcement of the Delaware choice-of-law provision.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado choice-of-law rules, potentially as interpreted under the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 (followed by many jurisdictions including Colorado) dictates that a contractual choice-of-law provision will not be enforced if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no reasonable basis for the parties' choice, or if application of the law of the chosen state would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue. Because the event is in Colorado, Colorado law could be applicable, despite the change.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The change from Colorado to Delaware law introduces a potential contradiction, as it may violate conflict of laws principles. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both describe that the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, followed by Colorado, states that a choice-of-law provision won't be enforced if the chosen state (Delaware) has no substantial relationship to the parties or transaction, or if applying Delaware law contradicts a fundamental policy of a state (Colorado) with a materially greater interest. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' If the event is in Colorado, and Colorado has a greater interest, Colorado law may apply despite the contract stating Delaware. This creates a legally contestable situation where the contractual provision regarding governing law might be deemed unenforceable, constituting a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be amended except in writing signed by a duly authorized representative of the respective Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended verbally by any participant at the event, regardless of whether they are a duly authorized representative of the respective Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that the agreement can be verbally amended by ANY participant directly contradicts the Statute of Frauds.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change allows for verbal amendments by any participant, contravening the original clause requiring written amendments signed by authorized representatives.  Colorado's Statute of Frauds (C.R.S. § 38-10-112) dictates that certain agreements, particularly those not to be performed within one year, must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged.  Allowing verbal amendments undermines this requirement, potentially rendering any verbal modification unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Colorado Revised Statutes Section 38-10-112 (2022) - Void Agreements :: 2022 Colorado Revised Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia Go to Other Versions of The Colorado Rev. Void Agreements Universal Citation: CO Rev Stat § 38-10-112 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Except for contracts for the sale of goods which are governed by section 4-2-201, C.R.S., and lease contracts which are governed by section 4-2.5-201, C.R.S., in the following cases every agreement shall be void, unless such agreement or some note or memorandum thereof is in writing and subscribed by the party charged therewith: Every agreement that by the terms is not to be performed within one year after the making thereof; Every special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another person; Every agreement, promise, or undertaking made upon consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry. Repealed. Source: R.S. p. 339, § 12. G.L. § 1262. G.S. § 1521. R.S. 08: § 2666. C.L. § 5111. CSA: C. 71, § 12. CRS 53: § 59-1-12. C.R.S. 1963: § 59-1-12. L. 69: p. 392, § 1. perturbation.justification: The modified text allows for verbal amendments by any participant, directly contradicting the original agreement which required written amendments signed by authorized representatives. This contradicts Colorado Revised Statutes Section 38-10-112, which states that 'every agreement that by the terms is not to be performed within one year after the making thereof... shall be void, unless such agreement or some note or memorandum thereof is in writing and subscribed by the party charged therewith'. Allowing verbal amendments undermines the requirement for written agreements for those agreements that fall under the statute of frauds, thus creating a legal contradiction.
